concepts.title: Gravitational Potential
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential

At a point in a gravitational field potential $\text{V}$ is defined as negative of the work done per unit mass in the shifting a rest mass from some reference point to the given point.

Change in gravitation potential is defined as change in potential energy of system per unit mass.

$\text{dV}=\frac{\text{d}U}{\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}}$

Reference: The work done in bringing a unit mass from infinity to a point in the gravitational field is called the 'gravitational potential' at the point.

Since, work is obtained hence it is negative. Hence gravitational potential is always negative at any point with respect to potential at infinity.

concepts.title: Gravitational Potential Due to Point Mass
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential Due to Point Mass 

Suppose a point mass $\text{M}$ is situated at a point $\text{O}$. We want to find the gravitational potential due to this mass at a point $\text{P}$ a distance $\text{r}$ from $\text{O}$. For this let us find work done in taking the unit mass from $\text{P}$ to infinity. This will be

$\text{W}=\mathop{\int }_{\text{r}}^{\infty }\text{Fdr}=\mathop{\int }_{\text{r}}^{\infty }\frac{\text{GM}}{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}\cdot \text{dr}=\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}$

Hence, the work done in bringing unit mass from infinity of $\text{P}$ will be $-\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}$. 

Thus, the gravitational potential at $\text{P}$ will be, $\text{V}=-\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}$

concepts.title: Gravitational Potential Due to Uniform Solid Sphere
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential Due to Uniform Solid Sphere

Potential at an external point : The gravitational potential due to a uniform sphere at an external point is same as that due to a single particle of same mass placed at its centre. Thus,

$\text{V}\left( \text{r} \right)=-\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{r}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ r}\ge \text{R}$

At the surface, $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r=R\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ and $V=-\frac{GM}{R}$

Potential at internal point : At some internal point, potential at a distance $r$ from the centre is given by,

$\text{V}\left( \text{r} \right)=\frac{\text{GM}}{{{\text{R}}^{3}}}\left( \frac{3}{2}{{\text{R}}^{2}}-\frac{{{\text{r}}^{2}}}{2} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ r}\le \text{R}$

At $\text{r}=\text{R},\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=-\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{R}}$

while at $\text{r}=0,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=-\frac{3\text{GM}}{2\text{R}}$

i.e., at the centre of the sphere the potential is 1.5 times the potential at surface. The variation of $\text{V}$ versus $\text{r}$ graph is as shown in Fig.

concepts.title: Gravitational Potential Due to Uniform Spherical Shell
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential Due to Uniform Spherical Shell

Potential at an external point: To calculate the potential at an external point, a uniform spherical shell may be treated as a point mass of same magnitude at its centre. Thus, potential at a distance $r$ is given by,

$V\left( r \right)=-\frac{GM}{r}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r\ge R$

$\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ at }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }r=R$ $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ V}=-\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{R}}$

 

Potential at an internal point: 

The potential due to a uniform spherical shell is constant throughout at any point inside the shell and this is equal to $-\frac{\text{GM}}{\text{R}}$.

concepts.title: Gravitational Potential Due to Uniform Circular Ring
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential Due to Uniform Circular Ring

The gravitational potential at a distance $r$ from the centre on the axis of a ring of mass $M$ and radius $R$ is given by,

$\text{V}\left( \text{r} \right)=-\frac{\text{GM}}{\sqrt{{{\text{R}}^{2}}+{{\text{r}}^{2}}}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0\le \text{r}\le \infty $

At $r=0,V=-\frac{GM}{R}$, i.e., at the centre of the ring gravitational potential is $-\frac{GM}{R}$

Gravitational potential is a field function. It depends on the position of the point where potential is desired. Gravitational field and the gravitational potential are related by the following relation.

\(\begin{aligned} \vec{E} & =-\operatorname{gradient} V=-\operatorname{grad} V \\ & =-\left[\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} \hat{i}+\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} \hat{j}+\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} \hat{k}\right] \\ \text { or } \vec{E} & =-\left[\frac{\partial V}{\partial x} \hat{i}+\frac{\partial V}{\partial y} \hat{j}+\frac{\partial V}{\partial z} \hat{k}\right]\end{aligned}\) … (i)

Here, $\frac{\partial \text{V}}{\partial \text{x}}=$ Partial derivative of potential function $\text{V}$ w.r.t. $\text{x}$, i.e., differentiate $\text{V}$ w.r.t. $\text{x}$ assuming $\text{y}$ and $\text{z}$ to be constant Eq. (i) can be written in following different forms.

(i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ E}=-\frac{\text{dV}}{\text{dx}}$, if gravitational field is along $\text{x}$-direction only

(ii) $\text{dV}=-\vec{E}\cdot \text{d}\vec{r}$,

Here \(d \vec{r}=d x \hat{i}+d y \hat{j}+d z \hat{k}\) and \(\vec{E}=E_x i+E_y j+E_z k\)

concepts.title: Gravitational Potential Energy of a System
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Potential Energy of a System 

Consider a system of two bodies A of mass ${{\text{m}}_{1}}$ and $\text{B}$ of mass ${{\text{m}}_{2}}$. Then its potential energy is defined by work done in assembling the system. To get its expression, lets consider that body A is fixed and B is moved from infinity to assemble system.

$ \begin{aligned} & {\mathrm{F}} \text { at distance } \mathrm{X} \text { on } \mathrm{m}_2 \text { due to } \\ & \mathrm{m}_1=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{x}^2} \\ & \mathrm{dW}=\vec{\mathrm{F}} \cdot \mathrm{dx}=\frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx} \\ & \mathrm{W}=\int \frac{\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{x}^2} \mathrm{dx}=\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2\left[\frac{-1}{\mathrm{x}}\right]_{\infty}^{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \mathrm{W}=\frac{-\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{r}} \\ & \end{aligned} $

Change in potential energy $=\frac{-\mathrm{Gm}_1 \mathrm{~m}_2}{\mathrm{r}}$ of the system. 

Tip: Whenever you move a body along the direction of force, the potential energy of system decreases.

concepts.title: Gravitational Self Energy
concepts.conceptBody:
Gravitational Self Energy 

Consider a sphere of radius R. During its formation, mass is assembled and in that process some work is involved and it tries to stabilise the system i.e. sphere. Hence potential energy is decreased and final potential energy of sphere of radius $R$ and mass $M$ is found by :

Consider a sphere of ' $\text{x}$ ' is directly formed and a shell of thickness ' $\text{dx}$ ' is added to it.

Change in potential energy of system

$=-\mathop{\int }_{0}^{R}G\frac{M{{x}^{3}}M4\pi {{x}^{2}}}{{{R}^{3}}x\left( 4/3 \right)\pi {{R}^{3}}}dx$

\(\begin{gathered}=\frac{-3 \mathrm{GM}^2}{\mathrm{R}^6}\left[\int \mathrm{x}^4 \mathrm{dx}\right]_0^{\mathrm{R}} \\ =\frac{-3 \mathrm{GM}^2 \mathrm{R}^5}{5 \mathrm{R}}=\frac{-3 \mathrm{GM}^2}{5 \mathrm{R}} \\ \Rightarrow \text { Potential energy of system }=\frac{-3 \mathrm{GM}^2}{5 \mathrm{R}}\end{gathered}\)

This energy is just due to sphere and hence it is called as self energy. 

Inside self potential energy of solid sphere $=\frac{-\text{G}{{\text{M}}^{2}}}{10\text{R}}$

concepts.title: Escape Velocity
concepts.conceptBody:
Escape Velocity 

It is the minimum velocity with which a body must be projected from the surface of earth so that it permanently overcomes and escapes the gravitational field of the earth. We can also say that a body projected with escape velocity will be able to go to a point which is at infinite distance from the earth.

Let us imagine what happens to a body of mass $m$ if it is thrown from the earth with a velocity ${{\text{V}}_{\text{e}}}$ (escape velocity). As the body moves away from the earth, it slows down (due to gravitational pull of the earth) and hence its kinetic energy is converted into gravitational potential energy of the mass-earth system. Let us imagine that is just able to reach upto infinity (where G.P.E. is zero).

K.E. lost by mass m = gain in G.P.E. of mass-earth system 

$\frac{1}{2}\text{mV}_{\text{e}}^{2}={{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ G}\text{.P}\text{.E}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ })}_{\text{f}}}-{{(\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ G}\text{.P}\text{.E}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ })}_{\text{i}}}$

\(\begin{array}{l}\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mV}_{\mathrm{e}}^2=0-\left(-\frac{\mathrm{GMm}}{\mathrm{R}}\right) \\ \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 \mathrm{GM}}{\mathrm{R}}} \text{ or } \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{e}}=\sqrt{2 \mathrm{gR}}\end{array}\)

Substituting the values of $\text{g}=9.81\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ m}/{{\text{s}}^{2}}$ and $\text{R}=6400\text{Km}$, we get :

${{V}_{e}}=11.3~km/s$

Hence any object thrown with a velocity of $11.3\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ km}/\text{s}$ or more will escape the gravitational field of the earth and will never come back to the earth.
